name: Quarry (Talc)
short_description: Historic talc quarry in the Adirondack foothills offering access to metamorphic minerals including talc, mica, and quartz specimens. Flat terrain makes this a moderate rockhounding site.
description:
The Talc Quarry near Town of Diana sits within the Adirondack Mountains of northern New York, a region famous for metamorphic mineral deposits. This site is part of the broader Lewis County talc belt, where Precambrian metamorphic rocks have been quarried and collected for over a century. The quarry exposes layers of talc schist interbanded with mica schist and quartzite, creating excellent opportunities to find talc specimens, muscovite and biotite mica, and clear to milky quartz crystals.

The relatively flat terrain at 815 feet elevation makes this one of the more accessible rockhounding locations in the Adirondack region. You can walk directly to exposed rock faces and work outcrops without significant elevation gain. The site's history as an active or recently worked quarry means fresh faces are frequently exposed, increasing your chances of finding quality specimens. Collectors should focus on the talc-rich zones, which often weather to a soft, pale white or greenish material that contrasts with harder surrounding rocks.

This location is best visited during warmer months when access roads are passable and the ground is not frozen. The flat slope and moderate difficulty make it suitable for families with older children and intermediate rockhounders. Always exercise caution around old quarry workings, as pit edges and loose material can be hazardous.
history: The Lewis County talc belt formed during the Grenville Orogeny approximately 1 billion years ago, when intense metamorphic pressure and heat transformed sedimentary and igneous protoliths into schist and gneiss. Talc deposits in this region were heavily quarried in the 20th century for industrial applications including cosmetics, paint, and ceramics, making New York a major talc producer.
tips: Search the exposed rock faces and rubble piles around the quarry perimeter for soft, pale green to white talc schist fragments, which often separate cleanly along foliation planes. Bring a geologist's hammer or chisel to carefully extract specimens from outcrop; do not use power tools or heavy machinery. Look for zones where silvery mica books are embedded in the talc matrix, as these combined specimens are prized by collectors. The flat terrain means you can cover a lot of ground, so systematically walk the quarry edges and examine all freshly exposed faces.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain at 815 feet elevation with minimal slope, making walking easy. The site is an old quarry with exposed rock faces, scattered talc schist blocks, and cleared work areas. Ground footing is firm but uneven due to quarried stone fragments and worked rock piles. No established trails; access is cross-country walking across relatively open ground. Vegetation is sparse to moderate in the immediate quarry area.
best_season: May - October. Summer and early fall provide the best access when roads are clear and the ground is dry. Winter snow and freeze-thaw cycles can make the site inaccessible and increase hazards from unstable rock.
written_directions: From Town of Diana, take NY Route 3 east toward Lowville approximately 5 miles. Watch for local quarry access roads or ask at the nearest store for current access information. The GPS coordinates (44.121674, -75.38171) mark the site location. Note that this is a historic quarry and current access may require permission from the property owner; contact Lewis County authorities or local rockhounding clubs for up-to-date access details.
road_conditions: Approach roads may be unpaved or rough. High-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle recommended depending on season and recent maintenance.
parking_notes: Limited or informal parking near the quarry site. No developed parking area. Arrive early to secure a safe spot away from active quarry operations.
collection_rules: This site is on private land and access requires prior permission from the property owner or local quarry operator. Collection is not permitted without explicit written consent. Do not trespass. Contact Lewis County or local rockhounding clubs for current access status and permission procedures.
rules:
Private property - permission required before visiting
No collection without owner consent
Stay clear of active mining operations
Do not remove large specimens or use powered equipment
Respect all posted no trespassing signs
Fill holes and leave area undisturbed
nearest_services: Town of Diana and Lowville approximately 5-8 miles away, with basic services including gas and food.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain is relatively accessible, but the site is an old quarry with uneven footing, loose rocks, and unstable edges. Not suitable for wheelchairs or walkers. Sturdy hiking boots required. Hazards from old quarry pit edges and unstable rock make this site unsuitable for very young children or those with limited mobility.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at the Talc Quarry near Diana, New York?
faq.answer: The primary find is talc in soft, pale greenish-white schist. You can also collect muscovite and biotite mica, often as silvery book-like crystals, plus clear to milky quartz. These minerals occur together in the metamorphic rocks that make up the quarry's exposed faces.

faq.question: Can I collect at the Diana Talc Quarry without a permit?
faq.answer: No. This is private property and requires explicit permission from the owner or quarry operator before you can legally collect. Contact Lewis County or local rockhounding clubs to inquire about current access and permission procedures.

faq.question: Is the Diana Talc Quarry beginner-friendly for rockhounding?
faq.answer: The flat terrain makes walking easy, but the site's private status and hazards from old quarry operations make it better suited for intermediate to advanced rockhounders who understand safety protocols. Beginners should seek public BLM or state forest alternatives first.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at this New York talc quarry?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best conditions, when roads are clear, the ground is dry, and the site is accessible. Winter freeze-thaw cycles and snow make the quarry hazardous and often inaccessible.

faq.question: How far is the Diana Talc Quarry from Lowville?
faq.answer: The quarry is approximately 7 miles from Lowville, the nearest town with services. From Lowville, take NY Route 3 east toward Town of Diana. Local rockhounding clubs and Lewis County can provide detailed directions and access information.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect talc and mica specimens at this quarry?
faq.answer: Bring a geologist's hammer, chisel, and cold chisel for carefully extracting specimens from soft talc schist. A magnifying glass helps identify mica books. Do not use power tools or heavy machinery. Always wear safety glasses and gloves when working quarry rock.

meta_title: Diana Talc Quarry Rockhounding - New York
meta_description: Collect talc, mica, and quartz at the historic Diana Talc Quarry in Lewis County, New York. Private site with permission required. Flat Adirondack terrain.
